blurb:
Squash is a two-and-a-half-year-old boxer mix, 57 pounds, and he's got that boxer face that makes it basically impossible to stay in a bad mood around him. Wrinkly, expressive, slightly ridiculous. You know the look.

His activity level is listed as moderate, which means he's not going to destroy your house if you miss a walk, but he's also not a dog who wants to spend the whole day on the couch. He gets along with other dogs, so if you've already got one at home, there's a decent chance Squash slides right in. His housetraining status is unknown since he came in without that history, but boxers are smart and motivated dogs. A consistent routine and a little patience go a long way with a breed like this.

Squash comes fully vaccinated, microchipped, neutered, and heartworm negative. That last part is worth noting since it means one less thing to think about after adoption day. He's ready to go as soon as the right person walks through the door.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy in Lewisville. Stop in during visiting hours to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Heartworm-negative status is a genuine competitive advantage worth highlighting — zero post-adoption medical friction
• Boxer face is a proven visual crowd-pleaser that converts newsletter browsers into shelter visitors faster than almost any other breed
• Unknown housetraining history suggests he arrived without a backstory, implying possible extended shelter time and quiet urgency

blurb:
Almost 80 pounds of shepherd mix, one year and four months old, already housetrained, and listed as high energy. Lefty is a lot of dog in the best possible way. If you have the space and the lifestyle to match him, he's probably going to be one of the best decisions you've ever made.

He's social with other dogs, though he takes his time warming up to new ones rather than rushing the introduction. That's not a red flag, that's just a dog with some self-awareness. He walks into a new situation and reads the room first. Give him a proper intro and he'll get there.

He has tested heartworm positive, but the shelter is covering his full treatment as part of the adoption. It's a common condition and very treatable. Call Lewisville Animal Services at the number below before you visit and they'll explain the treatment timeline so you know what you're signing up for. He comes vaccinated, microchipped, and neutered, and he's already got the housetraining piece figured out, which puts him ahead of a lot of dogs his age.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy. Walk in during visiting hours to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Heartworm-positive large shepherd mix combination historically means the longest shelter stays, making his placement genuinely time-sensitive
• Already housetrained at 16 months is a standout practical selling point that serious adopters will notice immediately
• 'Reads the room' social intelligence reframes his cautious dog introductions as a personality asset, not a liability

blurb:
His name is Hendrix, he's a two-year-old pit bull mix, and the shelter summed him up in one word: goofy. That tracks. You can see it in his face. He's the kind of dog who's probably tripped over his own feet at least once today and did not care even a little bit.

Hendrix loves to play and has a moderate-to-high activity level, so he's not a couch dog. He gets along with other dogs, though he plays on the rougher side, so a similarly sized dog who can hold their own would probably be a better match than a small, delicate one. He needs a family that's going to get outside with him and actually tire him out. If that sounds like your household, he's going to fit right in.

He has tested positive for heartworms, but that's not a dealbreaker. Lewisville Animal Services covers the treatment as part of the adoption, and heartworm disease is very manageable with the right protocol. Call the shelter at the number below and they'll walk you through exactly what to expect. He comes fully vaccinated, microchipped, and neutered.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy in Lewisville. Walk in during visiting hours to meet Hendrix in person.
scoring_notes:
• Heartworm-positive pit bull mix faces the steepest statistical adoption challenge, making his feature genuinely urgent
• 'Goofy' personality paired with a medical hurdle creates a heartwarming underdog story readers will want to resolve
• Shelter-covered treatment removes the financial barrier, giving the newsletter a strong call-to-action hook for hesitant adopters

blurb:
Rudy is not a lap cat. Not yet, anyway. He's a year and a half old, full of energy, and still figuring out that petting is a good thing and not a threat. He loves to play, though, and that's a solid foundation to build on.

The shelter is upfront that he needs someone with patience and a willingness to let him come around on his own schedule. That's not a knock on him. Some cats just need more time and space before they trust people, and the ones who do eventually warm up tend to bond really hard. If you're the kind of person who likes a project and appreciates a cat who earns your trust rather than just handing it over immediately, Rudy is worth a look.

He does well with other cats, so if you already have one at home, that could actually help him settle in faster. He's vaccinated, neutered, microchipped, and litterbox trained. Best in a home without young children.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy in Lewisville. Visit during shelter hours to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• 'Earns your trust' framing transforms a shy cat into a compelling challenge for the right reader
• Narrower adopter fit — no young kids, needs patience — means he likely sits longer and urgency is real
• The slow-trust-to-deep-bond arc resonates with experienced cat owners who are exactly the newsletter's engaged readership

blurb:
Luna has lived with kids, adults, seniors, dogs, and cats. She is basically the most flexible roommate imaginable. At just one year old and 8 pounds, she's young enough to adapt to almost any household, and she's already proven she can do it.

She took a little while to settle into the shelter, which is completely fair. Shelters are loud and strange and not exactly a cat's ideal environment. The staff describe her as independent, which usually means she'll be happy to hang out on her own terms rather than demanding constant attention. For a lot of people, that's actually exactly what they're looking for.

She's fully vaccinated, spayed, and microchipped. Litterbox trained, gets along with other animals, and has been around children before. She's the rare cat where almost any home could be the right one.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy in Lewisville. Stop by during visiting hours to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Widest possible adopter pool — compatible with kids, seniors, dogs, and cats makes her an easy yes for almost any reader
• Young, healthy, fully vetted, and low-maintenance independent personality checks every practical box
• Blurb hints at a settling-in period but gives no signals of a long wait, suggesting she is relatively new to the shelter

blurb:
Eight years on the street will toughen anybody up. Pacino knows that better than most. He's done the outside life, figured out it's overrated, and decided he's ready to be somebody's couch cat. The name fits, honestly. There's a presence to him.

But here's the thing about Pacino: the tough exterior is a bit of a front. The shelter describes him as a lover boy with a gentle meow, and that tracks. He's vaccinated, neutered, microchipped, and at 12 pounds, he is absolutely ready to be spoiled. He just needs someone willing to give him the soft life he's clearly earned.

They don't have a full read on how he does with kids or other animals yet, since he came in off the streets. A meet and greet at the shelter is the easiest way to figure out if he'd be a good fit for your house. Walk-ins are welcome during visiting hours.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy in Lewisville. Call ahead if you have questions or just show up and ask for Pacino.
scoring_notes:
• Street-to-couch redemption arc is irresistible newsletter gold with broad reader emotional appeal
• 'Tough exterior, lover boy inside' contrast gives readers a character worth rooting for
• Eight years on the streets implies a harder road to adoption, signaling genuine urgency to place him now
